## [00:00 - 03:00]

And now you've set it up to go into the teaching part of your webinar. So we spend a lot of time on the introduction, even though it's only really at most 15 minutes of your total presentation. So we hook, paint, excite, position, and now we move on to the content portion of the webinar. And we set this up with something called a paradigm. Now here is my definition of what I mean by paradigm. The paradigm to me that I'm going to teach to on a webinar is a conclusion that you want your audience to reach that is most conducive to them making a purchasing decision of your product. So if you want to think about it, whatever you're selling, say, what does somebody need to believe to be true that would make them buy this product? What is the thing more than anything that would need to be true? Once they understood this, they would buy this product or otherwise they would never buy this product. That's the paradigm. Now what we want to have happen in the presentation is for people to have this aha moment. We're somewhere during the content, during the training of your webinar, they say, oh my God, now that I know this, this changes everything because everybody coming to your webinar has a current paradigm that limits them, that prevents them from being successful. If they didn't have that, they wouldn't need you. They wouldn't need your webinar. They wouldn't need your product. They would already have the result. So they have some limitation and your new paradigm you're going to give them will remove those limitations. Here's the way that I think about it. You take a rubber band and you stretch it. That's what happens. It goes right back to the way it was. Unless you stretch it so far, you break it. Then you could never put it back together ever again. So we want to take their limits, stretch them so far apart that we break them and then they're like, I don't know what to do. And then you put them back together in a better way. They'll be more receptive to it. They'll be more excited about it and you'll actually help them. There are three techniques that I do more than any other on all the webinars for myself and my clients that help create these new paradigms. The first one is show the manual, sell the automatic. The second one is small results now are better than bigger results later. And the third is the ground floor. So I'm going to give you high level examples of what each of these could look like. So show the manual, sell the automatic. The old paradigm could be going into the webinar, okay, I know how to do this and I'm going to do this even though it's hard. People are walking in saying, I'm going to do this. I really am going to exercise and build a routine and a program to get to that fitness goal that I want to. Yeah, I know it's going to be tough. It's difficult. I've tried that. That's their current paradigm. And then what they discover is, wait, you'll do all of that for me? You'll count the calories for me? You'll set up and program my workouts for me? You'll figure out precisely and specifically based on my body type and based on my age and other restrictions what makes the most sense for me physically?

## [03:00 - 06:01]

Okay, sign me up. So they were going to do it anyway, but they think it's very difficult and challenging and hard and they're kind of like, yeah, I'll do it, but God, I don't know if I can really do it. That's a big commitment. Only for them to find out that most of it will be done for them. My favorite way to sell on a webinar is get people to agree that no matter how hard it is, they're committed to doing it, thinking they have to do it on their own, only to reveal that part or all of the offer later is done for them. There is no greater aha moment that, oh my God, this changes everything from I have to do everything myself to now I only have to do this or that or maybe these couple of things all on my own. So this is a very powerful framework for teaching on a webinar because you can teach your heart out because ultimately what you're going to teach them, they don't even have to do at the end of the day. They can appreciate how important it is and they can feel relieved to find out that the burden isn't no longer only on them. You could teach them how to change the oil in their car only to say, or we could do it for you. That's kind of the theory behind this. So this is a really powerful paradigm for training on a webinar. A second paradigm to consider is the small results now versus the big results later. So the old paradigm is like, oh my God, I got to be an absolute black belt expert at this. I got a slave away for a long time to get my dream outcome. I got to level up. I got to put in my thousand hours, my 10,000 hours to become an expert at this. And that's what they think moving into the webinar. But they leave saying, oh, I just got to have a little focused effort in this one specific way to get a small yet meaningful result. And that will not only improve the quality of my life, but it will create momentum and can compound into bigger wins later. And it's more believable. And then we train on that for 45 to 60 minutes on the webinar, because that's how long the content portion is. And so that's another paradigm that I use quite frequently. And a third one, my favorite one, although this is the one that you have the least control over, is where something happens and you help people get on the ground floor of a change or some disruption that's occurring in the industry that you're in. So generally, the paradigm of success is a long, slow, gradual process that requires a lot of work, effort, and expertise. And yet, if something shifts in the industry and you can put people on the ground floor of something new, then it's more about being in the right time in the right place. And the best pitch I ever did was take advantage of this time and this place right now and make the money and also then develop the skills. So this is, if we know 80-20 rule or the Pareto's principle or the law, the vital few, all of these things mean the same thing. A few inputs get a majority of the outputs. sell. So people need to know, I just got to focus on these three things. And if I do that and I'm good at it, I'll win. And then the rest of this stuff, it really doesn't matter. Now I'm going to give you a couple additional tips when it comes to teaching the mechanisms during your webinar presentation. Show not just tell. You want to do both. You want screenshots. You want visual elements. A picture is worth a thousand words and a million dollars. The right picture is. So don't just tell them what to do. Show what it looks like as well. It's actually easier to create presentations when you show and not only tell and it's more effective as well. Make things specific. Never leave things open-ended. So don't just say, do some of these things. Say these four things. Don't just say, there's a lot of buyers out there. There are two types of buyers out there. Focus on these three things. When I talked about traffic on Amazon, I said these are the two types of buyers on Amazon. So anything you teach, put a number around it. Otherwise, it's infinite because there could be a million considerations. So there could be a thousand of them and they seem open-ended and that makes people feel uncomfortable and unsure. So button everything down. These are the three things. These are the four things. These are the two things. These are the five things. And now they become manageable. And when people understand them and can fit them in a box, they're more likely to respond and follow them. And that's the end of the content section. We tie down that last mechanism and then we transition into making our offer. I think the transition is maybe the most underrated part of all of the webinar. I spend more effort and time on the transition than pretty much anywhere because we want to put people in a state of mind that is most conducive to being open to hearing our offer. We are changing from teaching and training on the webinar to selling.

## [15:08 - 18:10]

And we can screw that up very easily. We can get people all the way by it and then say, well, what happened? This feels like a different person now presenting to me than who it was before. get me my result that I wanted. And then we go to a lower offer. ## [30:20 - 33:24]

And lots of our bonuses go after very specific objections. If we know that 20% of our audience is caught up on X, we create a specific bonus that destroys X. Now the last thing, every webinar should have scarcity attached to it. There should be some compelling reason that would reward somebody for acting sooner and some penalty that they would incur by acting later. Now here's the thing about scarcity. Not only is it the greatest motivator of all time, not only does it create more action than anything else that exists, but there's only three types of scarcity. Either price goes up after a certain period of time or a certain quantity is sold and or bonuses go away after a certain period of time or quantity is sold and or the offer expires at a certain time. I use all of those. Price goes up, bonuses go away, offer comes off the table. A trifecta of scarcity.

## [42:27 - 45:31]

Now here's the thing about scarcity. You can't just say, hey, you need to buy this date, otherwise the offer is yanked off the table. You have to give a reason why. So here's some of the reasons why that I've used. My favorite one is to reward fast action takers. We find people that act quickly and with determination tend to be the most successful. We want to encourage and reward such behavior. So if you act before this date, you get the best price and you get all the bonuses. If you act after this date, then the price becomes this and the bonuses go away. So they're like, okay, I get why he's doing that because he's rewarding those of us who act fast. Sometimes it could be, hey, this is a special offer and if it was always available to everyone at this price, it wouldn't be special anymore. So I wanted to make it special for you and that works too. The best scarcity if you can pull it off is external conditions. Hey, I can only put 73 people in this room. Do you want to be one of the 73? That kind of things. Sometimes when we launch new products, because it's brand new and I'm looking to create success stories, I'm willing to offer it to you far below market value and people say, well, that makes sense.
